---
title: "12 · Fourier Series"
subtitle: "Every periodic function is a sum of sines and cosines"
lesson-id: "t6.fourier-series"
tier: 6
prerequisites: ["t5.sequences-series-convergence", "t3.unit-circle"]
unlocks: ["t6.fourier-transform"]
applied-domains: ["research", "ai-ml", "systems"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [T5 L14 — Convergence](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/14-sequences-series-convergence/index.qmd)
   and [T3 L08 — Unit Circle](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/08-unit-circle/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with $\sin, \cos$ and Euler's $e^{i\theta}$?
3. Recall that periodic functions repeat: $f(x + T) = f(x)$.
:::

## Touch

Pick a periodic wave — square, sawtooth, triangle, or a straight
line that repeats. The widget shows the recipe of pure sines and
cosines that builds it.

```{ojs}
viewof fsSel = Inputs.select(["square wave: only odd sines (1, 3, 5, ...)", "sawtooth wave: alternating sines", "triangle wave: odd cosines decaying as 1/n²", "f(x) = x on [−π, π]: pure sines"], {value: "square wave: only odd sines (1, 3, 5, ...)", label: "Wave:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let info;
  switch(fsSel) {
    case "square wave: only odd sines (1, 3, 5, ...)": info = "f(x) = (4/π) Σ sin((2k+1)x)/(2k+1) for k = 0, 1, 2, ... Coefficients decay as 1/n. Slow convergence; Gibbs phenomenon at jumps."; break;
    case "sawtooth wave: alternating sines": info = "f(x) = 2 Σ (−1)^(n+1) sin(nx)/n for n = 1, 2, ... 1/n decay. Discontinuous at endpoints."; break;
    case "triangle wave: odd cosines decaying as 1/n²": info = "Decays as 1/n²: faster convergence than discontinuous waves. Continuous but not smooth."; break;
    case "f(x) = x on [−π, π]: pure sines": info = "Antisymmetric: only sines. b_n = 2(−1)^(n+1)/n. Approximates the line via sinusoids."; break;
  }
  return html`<div style="margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:0.95rem; color:#c2185b; text-align:center;">${info}</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

## Wonder

1. Why can *any* periodic function be written as sines and cosines?
2. The coefficients $a_n, b_n$ — how do you compute them?
3. How fast does the series converge? (Smoother $f$ → faster
   convergence.)

## Discover

### Problem 1

The **Fourier coefficients** for $f$ on $[-\pi, \pi]$:
$$a_n = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \int_{-\pi}^\pi f(x) \cos(nx) \, dx, \quad b_n = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \int_{-\pi}^\pi f(x) \sin(nx) \, dx.$$

Compute $a_0$ for $f(x) = x$ on $[-\pi, \pi]$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$a_0 = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \int_{-\pi}^\pi x \, dx = 0$ (odd function over
symmetric interval).

So $f$ has no constant term. Symmetry helps: odd functions have only
sines, even functions only cosines.
</details>

### Problem 2

For $f(x) = x$ on $[-\pi, \pi]$, compute $b_n$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$b_n = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \int_{-\pi}^\pi x \sin(nx) \, dx$. Integrate by
parts.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\int x \sin(nx) dx = -x \cos(nx)/n + \sin(nx)/n^2$. Evaluate
$[-\pi, \pi]$:
$= -\pi \cos(n\pi)/n + \sin(n\pi)/n^2 - (\pi \cos(-n\pi)/n - \sin(-n\pi)/n^2)$
$= -2\pi (-1)^n / n + 0 = 2\pi (-1)^{n+1} / n$.

Divide by $\pi$: $b_n = 2(-1)^{n+1}/n$.

So $f(x) = x = \sum 2(-1)^{n+1} \sin(nx)/n = 2 \sin x - \sin 2x +
(2/3) \sin 3x - \ldots$

The line $y = x$ is a sum of sines!
</details>

### Problem 3

The **complex form** is much cleaner. Define $c_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}
\int_{-\pi}^\pi f(x) e^{-inx} \, dx$. Then
$$f(x) = \sum_{n = -\infty}^\infty c_n e^{inx}.$$

This is just the same series with $\cos, \sin \to e^{\pm i n x}$ via
Euler's formula.

Compute $c_n$ for $f(x) = e^{ix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$c_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^\pi e^{ix} e^{-inx} dx = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}
\int e^{i(1-n)x} dx$. For $n = 1$: integrand is 1, integral is
$2\pi$, $c_1 = 1$. For $n \neq 1$: integrand is $e^{i(1-n)x}$,
integral is 0.

So $c_n = \delta_{n, 1}$ — only $c_1 = 1$. Hence $f = c_1 e^{ix} =
e^{ix}$ ✓.

The complex Fourier series exhibits **orthogonality**: $\{e^{inx}\}$
are an orthonormal basis for periodic $L^2$ functions.
</details>

### Problem 4

**Parseval's theorem** (energy conservation):
$$\dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^\pi |f(x)|^2 \, dx = \sum_n |c_n|^2.$$

Verify for $f(x) = \cos x$ (so $c_1 = c_{-1} = 1/2$, others 0).

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
LHS: $\dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int \cos^2 x \, dx = 1/2$.

RHS: $|1/2|^2 + |1/2|^2 = 1/2$ ✓.

**Energy in the function = sum of squared coefficients.** A
generalisation of Pythagoras to function spaces.
</details>

### Problem 5

**Convergence rates.** For $f$ smooth, $|c_n|$ decays *faster* than
any polynomial in $1/n$. For $f$ with jumps: $|c_n| \sim 1/n$.

What does this say about *Fourier compression*?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Smooth signals compress well** (truncate after few terms; small
error). Discontinuous signals don't (need many terms).

**JPEG image compression** uses the Discrete Cosine Transform (a
Fourier cousin) on 8×8 blocks: smooth blocks → few coefficients
(small file). The mathematics of L12 → multi-billion-dollar
compression industry.

**MP3 audio:** Fourier-style decomposition into frequency components.
Drop inaudible coefficients → small file.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **periodic function** $f$ of period $2\pi$ has a **Fourier series**
$$f(x) = \dfrac{a_0}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^\infty (a_n \cos nx + b_n \sin nx)$$
with coefficients
$$a_n = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \int_{-\pi}^\pi f(x) \cos(nx) \, dx, \quad
b_n = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \int_{-\pi}^\pi f(x) \sin(nx) \, dx.$$

**Complex form:**
$$f(x) = \sum_{n = -\infty}^\infty c_n e^{inx}, \quad c_n =
\dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^\pi f(x) e^{-inx} \, dx.$$

**Convergence:** for piecewise smooth $f$, the series converges
pointwise to $f$ (and to the average at jumps).

**Parseval:** $\dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int |f|^2 \, dx = \sum |c_n|^2$ —
energy conservation.

**Symmetry:**

- Even $f$: only cosines.
- Odd $f$: only sines.

**Gibbs phenomenon:** truncated Fourier series at jumps overshoots by
~9% — never goes away with more terms.

**Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT):** discretised version, computed
efficiently by FFT in $O(n \log n)$.

:::

> *Periodic = sum of sines and cosines (Fourier);*
> *Coefficients via integration; energy conservation (Parseval);*
> *Smoother signals compress better.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Show the Fourier series of $f(x) = |x|$ on $[-\pi, \pi]$ has only
> cosine terms (and a constant).

$|x|$ is *even* (symmetric around 0). Hence $b_n = 0$ for all $n$
(integrand $|x| \sin nx$ is odd).

So $f(x) = a_0/2 + \sum a_n \cos(nx)$. Compute coefficients via
integration by parts.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Period of $\sin(2x)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\pi$.
</details>

**W2.** Even or odd: $f(x) = x^2$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Even.
</details>

**W3.** Fourier series of $f(x) = 5$ (constant) on $[-\pi, \pi]$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f(x) = 5$. $a_0 = 10$, $a_n = b_n = 0$ for $n \ge 1$. So series is
just the constant 5. ✓
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** $f(x) = \sin x + 3 \sin 2x + 2 \cos 5x$. Identify $a_5, b_1,
b_2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$a_5 = 2, b_1 = 1, b_2 = 3$. Others zero.
</details>

**P2.** Fourier series of $f(x) = \sin^2 x$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sin^2 x = (1 - \cos 2x)/2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f = 1/2 - (1/2) \cos 2x$. So $a_0 = 1, a_2 = -1/2$, others zero.
**Already a finite Fourier series.**
</details>

**P3.** Why does an even function have no sine coefficients?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f$ even, $\sin$ odd → $f \sin$ odd → integral over symmetric
interval is 0.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **The Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT).** Given $N$ samples
$x_0, \ldots, x_{N-1}$:
$$X_k = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_n e^{-2\pi i kn/N}.$$

The **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** computes this in $O(N \log N)$
instead of $O(N^2)$. Why is this revolutionary?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**FFT (Cooley-Tukey, 1965)** is one of the most important algorithms
in computing. For $N = 10^6$: $N^2 = 10^{12}$ ops vs $N \log N
\approx 2 \cdot 10^7$ ops — **50000× faster**.

Applications enabled: digital audio processing (DSP), image
compression (JPEG), seismic analysis, MRI reconstruction, OFDM
(modern wireless: 4G, 5G, Wi-Fi).

A sub-percent of CS researchers can claim an algorithm with this
much real-world impact. FFT belongs to that elite list.
</details>

**S2.** **Why are convolutional neural networks called "convolutional"?**
The convolution operation is closely tied to Fourier transforms.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Convolution theorem:** convolution in the time/space domain equals
multiplication in the frequency domain (and vice versa).

CNNs apply *learned* convolution filters across an image. Each
filter detects a feature (edge, blob, etc.). The Fourier perspective:
filters are *learned spectral selectors*.

Modern computer vision works because:
1. Convolution exploits translational symmetry (Tier 6 L03 group
   actions).
2. Fourier theory gives compact representations (CNNs avoid
   transmitting all pixels).

Group convolutions (Tier 7) generalise to other symmetry groups
(rotation, scaling).
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Fourier needs sinusoidal data."** No — *any* periodic function
   decomposes into sinusoids.
2. **"Convergence is automatic."** Subtle issues — Gibbs phenomenon,
   conditional vs uniform convergence. Tier 7 functional analysis
   resolves carefully.
3. **"DFT and FFT are the same."** DFT is the *transformation*; FFT
   is the *fast algorithm* to compute it.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you explain why even functions have only cosine
   coefficients?
2. Without scrolling back, what does Parseval's theorem state?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int |f|^2 = \sum |c_n|^2$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[13 · Fourier Transform](../13-fourier-transform/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (FFT).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P2 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T5 L14 — Convergence](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/14-sequences-series-convergence/index.qmd)**
- **[T3 L08 — Unit Circle](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/08-unit-circle/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- **[13 · Fourier Transform](../13-fourier-transform/index.qmd)**

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** CNNs (S2). Spectral analysis of neural
networks. Transformers' positional encodings use Fourier features.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Engineering.** DSP, audio (MP3), image (JPEG), video (MPEG).
Wireless (OFDM). Radar, MRI.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Fourier analysis is *the* tool for
periodic and quasi-periodic phenomena. Generalises to harmonic
analysis on groups.
:::
